title: practical english usage - Part 43
content:
This cover is not to be removed.

am/are/is (not) to be + past participle Be + passive infinitive is often used in notices and instructions.

5  be   + passive      infinitive:      It is  not    to   be   removed.

She can go to the party, but she's not to be back late.

You are to do your homework before you watch TV.

The  structure is used to give orders, for example by parents speaking to children.

4 orders:    You     are    to   do   your      homework.

He knew he would have to work hard if he was to pass his exam.

If we are to get there by lunchtime, we had better hurry.

condition - something that must happen first if something else is to happen.

This structure is common in if-c lauses, when the main clause expresses a pre­ 3   pre-conditions:         If  we    are    to   get    there      in   time     .. .

years later, under very strange circumstances.

I thought we were saying goodbye for ever. But we were to meet again, many to happen.

Another use is to talk about things which  are/were 'hidden in the future', fated 2 'fate':  We    were      to   meet      again.

I was to have started work last week, but I changed my mind.

A  perfect infinitive can be used to show that a planned event did not happen.

I felt nervous because I was soon to leave home for the first time.

We are to get a 10 per cent wage rise in June.

The President is to visit Nigeria next month.

arrangements.

We   use this structure in a formal style to talk about official and other plans and 1  plans    and   arrangements:         He  is  to   visit     Nigeria.

you     are    to   ..., etc 42 be   to   + infinitive:      I am    to   ..., grammar •  43   future in the past For was to have + past participle (e.g. She was to have taken over my job, but she fell ill), ►    42.1.

I've been going to write to you for ages, but I've only just found time.

Perfect forms of be going to are also possible.

I went to have a look at the room where I was to talk that afternoon.

In 1988 I arrived in the town where I would spend ten years of my life.

1 had no time to shop because I was leaving for Germany in two hours.

Last time I saw you, you were going to start a new job.

progressive; instead of will we  use would; instead of is to we  use was to.

we use was going to; instead of the present progressive we  use the past (►  35-42), but we  make the verb forms past. For example, instead of is going to this idea, we  use the structures that are normally used to talk about the future which  was in the future at that time - which  had not yet happened. To  express Sometimes when we  are talking about the past, we  want to talk about something 43 future     in  the   past          _______ Section 4 Talking     about     the   Future grammar •  Section 5  Past and Perfect Tenses used in exactly the same ways (►   48.2).

languages (e.g. j’ai vu, ich habe gesehen, ho visto, jeg har set), but that it is not constructed in the same way as a tense in some other Western European Note in particular that the English present perfect (e.g. I have seen) is The  various uses of these six verb forms are covered in the following entries.

verb forms which  show time.

time. In more practical books like this one, it is convenient to use 'tense' for all for example, whether an event is seen as ongoing or completed at a particular past) and 'aspect’  (perfective and progressive). Tense shows time; aspect shows, In academic grammars, a distinction is often made between 'tense’  (present or past perfect progressive (or 'continuous’ )              I had been working (simple) past perfect                                         I had worked present perfect progressive (or 'continuous’ )       I have been working (simple) present perfect                                  I have worked past progressive (or ’ continuous’ )                            I was working simple past                                                         I worked NAME                              EXAMPLE They  are: English has six different verb forms to refer to past events and situations.
